key_insight: Static equilibrium notions (Nash, correlated, and coarse correlated) can hide dynamic instability, and the paper argues that this can make classic Price of Anarchy guarantees misleading or even unbounded.
engineering_relevance: For multi-agent systems and networked optimization, using only equilibrium-based worst-case bounds may overestimate robustness and miss chaotic or unstable learning behavior.
method: Analyze the geometry of Nash equilibria and show interior equilibria can lack usable $C^1$ vector-field information.
Study how PoA behaves under strictly positive affine costs, dominated strategies, and congestion-game dynamics.
Examine learning dynamics beyond static equilibrium, including swap-regret, proximal refinements, and non-atomic congestion-game limits.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports theoretical results across canonical congestion games and non-atomic congestion-game limits, including unbounded PoA under positive affine costs and chaotic/unstable long-run learning dynamics.
watch_item: These results are asymptotic and theory-driven, so applying them to finite, practical multi-agent systems may require checking whether the same instability mechanisms appear in the specific game and learning rule.
paper_title: Paradoxes of Game Theoretic Equilibria and Price of Anarchy
